revision.text: #redirect[[en:Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Yanaidanu-no-Oyama Kofun | native_name = 柳田布尾山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Nunoo zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = | map_type = Japan Toyama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Yanaidanu-no-Oyama | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|49|24|N|136|59|27|E||display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Himi, Toyama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Hokuriku region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th or early 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = <!-- actually displays as "Periods" --> | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = 1998-2001 | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = National Historic Site | management = | public_access = Yes | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = }} {{nihongo|'''Yanaidanu-no-Oyama Kofun'''|柳田布尾山古墳|}} is a keyhole-shaped ''[[kofun]]'' [[burial mound]] located in the Yanaida neighborhood of the city of [[Himi, Toyama]] in the [[Hokuriku region]] of [[Japan]]. It has been protected by the central government as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 2001.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/140287 |title=柳田布尾山古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 December 2017}}</ref>
==Outline== The Yanaidanu-no-Oyama Kofun is located in the northwest part of Toyama prefecture, near the eastern base of [[Noto Peninsula]] and near the shore of [[Toyama Bay]], at an altitude of approximately 25 meters above sea level. With a length of 107.5 meters, it is the largest keyhole-shaped tumulus in Toyama Prefecture, and the 10th largest in the country. It was discovered only in 1998, and was excavated from 1998-2001. No ''[[haniwa]]'' or ''[[fukiishi]]'' were discovered on the kofun, which was found to have been robbed at some time in antiquity. The time of its construction is estimated to date from the end of the 3rd to the late 4th century, and there is no indication of who was buried in this tomb. The area is now open to the public as part of an archaeological park.
*Total length: 107.5 meters *Main Portion: 2-stages, 54 meter length x 53 meter width x 10 meter height *Keyway Portion: 2-stages, 53.5 meter length x 49 meter width x 6 meter height
The tumulus was constructed by partly cutting away the round on a narrow terrace, such that the embankment volume is about 60% of the total volume of the tumulus. The tumulus was surrounded by a amorphous moat measuring 5 to 18 meters wide and 1.2 to 1.2 meters deep. The tomb appears to be built on the site of a [[Yayoi period]] settlement, as pottery from that period has been found in the area.
There is a smaller (25 meter diameter) circular tumulus surrounded by a five-meter wide and 1.6 meter deep moat is located on the eastern rear side of the tomb. Details of its construction are unknown as it has not been excavated: however, from the details of its construction, there is a possibility that it predates the keyhole-shaped tomb.
==See also== {{Portal|Archaeology|Japan}} *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Toyama)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons category-inline}} * [https://www.info-toyama.com/spot/21018/ Toyama Tourist Information] {{ja}} *[http://www.city.himi.toyama.jp/shimin/node_26855/node_1342/node_1602/node_27824 Himi City official site] {{ja}}
[[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:History of Toyama Prefecture]] [[Category:Himi, Toyama]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]]

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun | native_name = 柳田布尾山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Nunoo zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Toyama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|49|24|N|136|59|27|E||display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Himi, Toyama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Hokuriku region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th or early 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = 1998-2001 | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park and museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun'''|柳田布尾山古墳|}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[burial mound]] located in the Yanaida neighborhood of the city of [[Himi, Toyama]] in the [[Hokuriku region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus has been protected as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 2001.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/140287 |title=柳田布尾山古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 December 2017}}</ref>
==Outline== The Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun is located in the northwest part of Toyama prefecture, near the eastern base of [[Noto Peninsula]] and approximately two kilometers from the shore of [[Toyama Bay]], at an elevation of approximately 25 meters above sea level. With a length of 107.5 meters, it is the largest "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōhō-fun''|前方後方墳||}} on the [[Sea of Japan]] coast, and the 10th largest in the country. It was discovered only in 1998, and was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] from 1998 to 2001. No ''[[haniwa]]'' or ''[[fukiishi]]'' were discovered , and the tumulus was found to have been robbed at some time in antiquity. The time of its construction is estimated to date from the end of the 3rd to the late 4th century, and there is no indication of who was buried in this tomb. Keyhole-shaped tombs are commonly attributed to a connection with the Yamato kingdom, whereas "two conjoined rectangle"-type tumuli are found more commonly in western Japan, where there is a connection to the [[Izumo Province|Izumo Kingdom]]. From its size and its location near an ancient port, it has been speculated that this was the tomb of a powerful chief with connections through trade to both polities. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
*Total length: 107.5 meters *Main Portion: 2-stages, 54 meter length x 53 meter width x 10 meter height *Keyway Portion: 2-stages, 53.5 meter length x 49 meter width x 6 meter height
The tumulus was constructed by partly cutting away the ground on a narrow terrace, such that the embankment volume is about 60% of the total volume of the tumulus. It is orientated to the north-northwest. The tumulus was surrounded by an amorphous moat measuring 5 to 18 meters wide and 1.2 to 1.2 meters deep. The tomb appears to be built on the site of a [[Yayoi period]] settlement, as [[Yayoi pottery]] has been found in the area. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
There is a smaller (25 meter diameter) circular tumulus (円墳) surrounded by a five-meter wide and 1.6 meter deep moat is located on the eastern rear side of the tomb. Details of its construction are unknown as it has not been excavated. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The area is now open to the public as part of an [[archaeological park]] with a small museum. It is about 10 minutes by car from [[Himi Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Himi Line]]. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun, kouhoubu.jpg|From front looking toward rear File:Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|From rear looking towards front File:Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun, rikkyo.jpg|Bridge across moat on east side File:Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun-2, zenkei.jpg|No.2 tumulus </gallery>

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun | native_name = 柳田布尾山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Nunoo zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Toyama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|49|24|N|136|59|27|E||display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Himi, Toyama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Hokuriku region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th or early 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = 1998-2001 | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park and museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun'''|柳田布尾山古墳|}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[burial mound]] located in the Yanaida neighborhood of the city of [[Himi, Toyama]] in the [[Hokuriku region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus has been protected as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 2001.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/140287 |title=柳田布尾山古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=ja|access-date=25 December 2017}}</ref>
==Outline== The Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun is located in the northwest part of Toyama prefecture, near the eastern base of [[Noto Peninsula]] and approximately two kilometers from the shore of [[Toyama Bay]], at an elevation of approximately 25 meters above sea level. With a length of 107.5 meters, it is the largest "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳}}''|前方後方墳}} on the [[Sea of Japan]] coast, and the 10th largest in the country. It was discovered only in 1998, and was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] from 1998 to 2001. No ''[[haniwa]]'' or ''[[fukiishi]]'' were discovered , and the tumulus was found to have been robbed at some time in antiquity. The time of its construction is estimated to date from the end of the 3rd to the late 4th century, and there is no indication of who was buried in this tomb. Keyhole-shaped tombs are commonly attributed to a connection with the Yamato kingdom, whereas "two conjoined rectangle"-type tumuli are found more commonly in western Japan, where there is a connection to the [[Izumo Province|Izumo Kingdom]]. From its size and its location near an ancient port, it has been speculated that this was the tomb of a powerful chief with connections through trade to both polities. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
*Total length: 107.5 meters *Main Portion: 2-stages, 54 meter length x 53 meter width x 10 meter height *Keyway Portion: 2-stages, 53.5 meter length x 49 meter width x 6 meter height
The tumulus was constructed by partly cutting away the ground on a narrow terrace, such that the embankment volume is about 60% of the total volume of the tumulus. It is orientated to the north-northwest. The tumulus was surrounded by an amorphous moat measuring 5 to 18 meters wide and 1.2 to 1.2 meters deep. The tomb appears to be built on the site of a [[Yayoi period]] settlement, as [[Yayoi pottery]] has been found in the area. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
There is a smaller (25 meter diameter) {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular tumulus|ja|円墳}} (円墳) surrounded by a five-meter wide and 1.6 meter deep moat is located on the eastern rear side of the tomb. Details of its construction are unknown as it has not been excavated. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The area is now open to the public as part of an [[archaeological park]] with a small museum. It is about 10 minutes by car from [[Himi Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Himi Line]]. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun, kouhoubu.jpg|From front looking toward rear File:Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|From rear looking towards front File:Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun, rikkyo.jpg|Bridge across moat on east side File:Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun-2, zenkei.jpg|No.2 tumulus </gallery>

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun | native_name = 柳田布尾山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Nunoo zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Toyama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|49|24|N|136|59|27|E||display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Himi, Toyama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Hokuriku region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th or early 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = 1998-2001 | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park and museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun'''|柳田布尾山古墳|}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[burial mound]] located in the Yanaida neighborhood of the city of [[Himi, Toyama]] in the [[Hokuriku region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus has been protected as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 2001.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/140287 |title=柳田布尾山古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=ja|access-date=25 December 2017}}</ref>
==Outline== The Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun is located in the northwest part of Toyama prefecture, near the eastern base of [[Noto Peninsula]] and approximately two kilometers from the shore of [[Toyama Bay]], at an elevation of approximately 25 meters above sea level. With a length of 107.5 meters, it is the largest "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳}}''|前方後方墳}} on the [[Sea of Japan]] coast, and the 10th largest in the country. It was discovered only in 1998, and was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] from 1998 to 2001. No ''[[haniwa]]'' or ''[[fukiishi]]'' were discovered , and the tumulus was found to have been robbed at some time in antiquity. The time of its construction is estimated to date from the end of the 3rd to the late 4th century, and there is no indication of who was buried in this tomb. Keyhole-shaped tombs are commonly attributed to a connection with the Yamato kingdom, whereas "two conjoined rectangle"-type tumuli are found more commonly in western Japan, where there is a connection to the [[Izumo Province|Izumo Kingdom]]. From its size and its location near an ancient port, it has been speculated that this was the tomb of a powerful chief with connections through trade to both polities. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
*Total length: 107.5 meters *Main Portion: 2-stages, 54 meter length x 53 meter width x 10 meter height *Keyway Portion: 2-stages, 53.5 meter length x 49 meter width x 6 meter height
The tumulus was constructed by partly cutting away the ground on a narrow terrace, such that the embankment volume is about 60% of the total volume of the tumulus. It is orientated to the north-northwest. The tumulus was surrounded by an amorphous moat measuring 5 to 18 meters wide and 1.2 to 1.2 meters deep. The tomb appears to be built on the site of a [[Yayoi period]] settlement, as [[Yayoi pottery]] has been found in the area. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
There is a smaller (25 meter diameter) {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular tumulus|ja|円墳}} (円墳) surrounded by a five-meter wide and 1.6 meter deep moat is located on the eastern rear side of the tomb. Details of its construction are unknown as it has not been excavated. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The area is now open to the public as part of an [[archaeological park]] with a small museum. It is about 10 minutes by car from [[Himi Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Himi Line]]. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun, kouhoubu.jpg|From front looking toward rear File:Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|From rear looking towards front File:Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun, rikkyo.jpg|Bridge across moat on east side File:Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun-2, zenkei.jpg|No.2 tumulus </gallery>
==See also== {{Portal|History|Japan}} *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Toyama)]]

The tomb appears to be built on the site of a [[Yayoi period]] settlement, as [[Yayoi pottery]] has been found in the area. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
There is a smaller (25 meter diameter) {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular tumulus|ja|円墳}} (円墳) surrounded by a five-meter wide and 1.6 meter deep moat is located on the eastern rear side of the tomb. Details of its construction are unknown as it has not been excavated. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The area is now open to the public as part of an [[archaeological park]] with a small museum. It is about 10 minutes by car from [[Himi Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Himi Line]]. <ref name="Shiseki"/>

The tumulus has been protected as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 2001.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/140287 |title=柳田布尾山古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=ja|access-date=25 December 2017}}</ref>

With a length of 107.5 meters, it is the largest "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳}}''|前方後方墳}} on the [[Sea of Japan]] coast, and the 10th largest in the country. The time of its construction is estimated to date from the end of the 3rd to the late 4th century, and there is no indication of who was buried in this tomb. Keyhole-shaped tombs are commonly attributed to a connection with the Yamato kingdom, whereas "two conjoined rectangle"-type tumuli are found more commonly in western Japan, where there is a connection to the [[Izumo Province|Izumo Kingdom]]. From its size and its location near an ancient port, it has been speculated that this was the tomb of a powerful chief with connections through trade to both polities. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun | native_name = 柳田布尾山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Nunoo zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Toyama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|49|24|N|136|59|27|E||display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Himi, Toyama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Hokuriku region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th or early 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = 1998-2001 | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park and museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun'''|柳田布尾山古墳|}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[burial mound]] located in the Yanaida neighborhood of the city of [[Himi, Toyama]] in the [[Hokuriku region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus has been protected as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 2001.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/140287 |title=柳田布尾山古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=ja|access-date=25 December 2017}}</ref>
==Outline== The Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun is located in the northwest part of Toyama prefecture, near the eastern base of [[Noto Peninsula]] and approximately two kilometers from the shore of [[Toyama Bay]], at an elevation of approximately 25 meters above sea level. With a length of 107.5 meters, it is the largest "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}''|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} on the [[Sea of Japan]] coast, and the 10th largest in the country. It was discovered only in 1998, and was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] from 1998 to 2001. No ''[[haniwa]]'' or ''[[fukiishi]]'' were discovered , and the tumulus was found to have been robbed at some time in antiquity. The time of its construction is estimated to date from the end of the 3rd to the late 4th century, and there is no indication of who was buried in this tomb. Keyhole-shaped tombs are commonly attributed to a connection with the Yamato kingdom, whereas "two conjoined rectangle"-type tumuli are found more commonly in western Japan, where there is a connection to the [[Izumo Province|Izumo Kingdom]]. From its size and its location near an ancient port, it has been speculated that this was the tomb of a powerful chief with connections through trade to both polities. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
*Total length: 107.5 meters *Main Portion: 2-stages, 54 meter length x 53 meter width x 10 meter height *Keyway Portion: 2-stages, 53.5 meter length x 49 meter width x 6 meter height
The tumulus was constructed by partly cutting away the ground on a narrow terrace, such that the embankment volume is about 60% of the total volume of the tumulus. It is orientated to the north-northwest. The tumulus was surrounded by an amorphous moat measuring 5 to 18 meters wide and 1.2 to 1.2 meters deep. The tomb appears to be built on the site of a [[Yayoi period]] settlement, as [[Yayoi pottery]] has been found in the area. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
There is a smaller (25 meter diameter) {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular tumulus|ja|円墳}} (円墳) surrounded by a five-meter wide and 1.6 meter deep moat is located on the eastern rear side of the tomb. Details of its construction are unknown as it has not been excavated. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The area is now open to the public as part of an [[archaeological park]] with a small museum. It is about 10 minutes by car from [[Himi Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Himi Line]]. <ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun | native_name = 柳田布尾山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Nunoo zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Toyama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|49|24|N|136|59|27|E||display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Himi, Toyama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Hokuriku region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th or early 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = 1998-2001 | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park and museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun'''|柳田布尾山古墳|}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[burial mound]] located in the Yanaida neighborhood of the city of [[Himi, Toyama]] in the [[Hokuriku region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus has been protected as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 2001.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/140287 |title=柳田布尾山古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=ja|access-date=25 December 2017}}</ref>
==Outline== The Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun is located in the northwest part of Toyama prefecture, near the eastern base of [[Noto Peninsula]] and approximately two kilometers from the shore of [[Toyama Bay]], at an elevation of approximately 25 meters above sea level. With a length of 107.5 meters, it is the largest "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}''|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} on the [[Sea of Japan]] coast, and the 10th largest in the country. It was discovered only in 1998, and was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] from 1998 to 2001. No ''[[haniwa]]'' or ''[[fukiishi]]'' were discovered , and the tumulus was found to have been robbed at some time in antiquity. The time of its construction is estimated to date from the end of the 3rd to the late 4th century, and there is no indication of who was buried in this tomb. Keyhole-shaped tombs are commonly attributed to a connection with the Yamato kingdom, whereas "two conjoined rectangle"-type tumuli are found more commonly in western Japan, where there is a connection to the [[Izumo Province|Izumo Kingdom]]. From its size and its location near an ancient port, it has been speculated that this was the tomb of a powerful chief with connections through trade to both polities. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
*Total length: 107.5 meters *Main Portion: 2-stages, 54 meter length x 53 meter width x 10 meter height *Keyway Portion: 2-stages, 53.5 meter length x 49 meter width x 6 meter height
The tumulus was constructed by partly cutting away the ground on a narrow terrace, such that the embankment volume is about 60% of the total volume of the tumulus. It is orientated to the north-northwest. The tumulus was surrounded by an amorphous moat measuring 5 to 18 meters wide and 1.2 to 1.2 meters deep. The tomb appears to be built on the site of a [[Yayoi period]] settlement, as [[Yayoi pottery]] has been found in the area. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
There is a smaller (25 meter diameter) {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} (円墳) surrounded by a five-meter wide and 1.6 meter deep moat is located on the eastern rear side of the tomb. Details of its construction are unknown as it has not been excavated. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The area is now open to the public as part of an [[archaeological park]] with a small museum. It is about 10 minutes by car from [[Himi Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Himi Line]]. <ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun | native_name = 柳田布尾山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Nunoo zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Toyama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|49|24|N|136|59|27|E||display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Himi, Toyama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Hokuriku region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th or early 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = 1998-2001 | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park and museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun'''|柳田布尾山古墳|}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[burial mound]] located in the Yanaida neighborhood of the city of [[Himi, Toyama]] in the [[Hokuriku region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus has been protected as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 2001.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/140287 |title=柳田布尾山古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=ja|access-date=25 December 2017}}</ref>
==Outline== The Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun is located in the northwest part of Toyama prefecture, near the eastern base of [[Noto Peninsula]] and approximately two kilometers from the shore of [[Toyama Bay]], at an elevation of approximately 25 meters above sea level. With a length of 107.5 meters, it is the largest "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}''|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} on the [[Sea of Japan]] coast, and the 10th largest in the country. It was discovered only in 1998, and was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] from 1998 to 2001. No ''[[haniwa]]'' or ''[[fukiishi]]'' were discovered , and the tumulus was found to have been robbed at some time in antiquity. The time of its construction is estimated to date from the end of the 3rd to the late 4th century, and there is no indication of who was buried in this tomb. Keyhole-shaped tombs are commonly attributed to a connection with the Yamato kingdom, whereas "two conjoined rectangle"-type tumuli are found more commonly in western Japan, where there is a connection to the [[Izumo Province|Izumo Kingdom]]. From its size and its location near an ancient port, it has been speculated that this was the tomb of a powerful chief with connections through trade to both polities. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
*Total length: 107.5 meters *Main Portion: 2-stages, 54 meter length x 53 meter width x 10 meter height *Keyway Portion: 2-stages, 53.5 meter length x 49 meter width x 6 meter height
The tumulus was constructed by partly cutting away the ground on a narrow terrace, such that the embankment volume is about 60% of the total volume of the tumulus. It is orientated to the north-northwest. The tumulus was surrounded by an amorphous moat measuring 5 to 18 meters wide and 1.2 to 1.2 meters deep. The tomb appears to be built on the site of a [[Yayoi period]] settlement, as [[Yayoi pottery]] has been found in the area. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
There is a smaller (25 meter diameter) {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} (円墳) surrounded by a five-meter wide and 1.6 meter deep moat is located on the eastern rear side of the tomb. Details of its construction are unknown as it has not been excavated. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The area is now open to the public as part of an [[archaeological park]] with a small museum. It is about 10 minutes by car from [[Himi Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Himi Line]]. <ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun | native_name = 柳田布尾山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Nunoo zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Toyama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|49|24|N|136|59|27|E||display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Himi, Toyama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Hokuriku region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th or early 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = 1998-2001 | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park and museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun'''|柳田布尾山古墳|}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]] located in the Yanaida neighborhood of the city of [[Himi, Toyama]] in the [[Hokuriku region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus has been protected as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 2001.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/140287 |title=柳田布尾山古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=ja|access-date=25 December 2017}}</ref>
==Outline== The Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun is located in the northwest part of Toyama prefecture, near the eastern base of [[Noto Peninsula]] and approximately two kilometers from the shore of [[Toyama Bay]], at an elevation of approximately 25 meters above sea level. With a length of 107.5 meters, it is the largest "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}''|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} on the [[Sea of Japan]] coast, and the 10th largest in the country. It was discovered only in 1998, and was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] from 1998 to 2001. No ''[[haniwa]]'' or ''[[fukiishi]]'' were discovered , and the tumulus was found to have been robbed at some time in antiquity. The time of its construction is estimated to date from the end of the 3rd to the late 4th century, and there is no indication of who was buried in this tomb. Keyhole-shaped tombs are commonly attributed to a connection with the Yamato kingdom, whereas "two conjoined rectangle"-type tumuli are found more commonly in western Japan, where there is a connection to the [[Izumo Province|Izumo Kingdom]]. From its size and its location near an ancient port, it has been speculated that this was the tomb of a powerful chief with connections through trade to both polities. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
*Total length: 107.5 meters *Main Portion: 2-stages, 54 meter length x 53 meter width x 10 meter height *Keyway Portion: 2-stages, 53.5 meter length x 49 meter width x 6 meter height
The tumulus was constructed by partly cutting away the ground on a narrow terrace, such that the embankment volume is about 60% of the total volume of the tumulus. It is orientated to the north-northwest. The tumulus was surrounded by an amorphous moat measuring 5 to 18 meters wide and 1.2 to 1.2 meters deep. The tomb appears to be built on the site of a [[Yayoi period]] settlement, as [[Yayoi pottery]] has been found in the area. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
There is a smaller (25 meter diameter) {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} (円墳) surrounded by a five-meter wide and 1.6 meter deep moat is located on the eastern rear side of the tomb. Details of its construction are unknown as it has not been excavated. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The area is now open to the public as part of an [[archaeological park]] with a small museum. It is about 10 minutes by car from [[Himi Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Himi Line]]. <ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun}} | name = Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun | native_name = 柳田布尾山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Nunoo zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Toyama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|49|24|N|136|59|27|E||display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Himi, Toyama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Hokuriku region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th or early 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = 1998-2001 | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park and museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun'''|柳田布尾山古墳|}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]] located in the Yanaida neighborhood of the city of [[Himi, Toyama]] in the [[Hokuriku region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus has been protected as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 2001.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/140287 |title=柳田布尾山古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=ja|access-date=25 December 2017}}</ref>
==Outline== The Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun is located in the northwest part of Toyama prefecture, near the eastern base of [[Noto Peninsula]] and approximately two kilometers from the shore of [[Toyama Bay]], at an elevation of approximately 25 meters above sea level. With a length of 107.5 meters, it is the largest "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}''|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} on the [[Sea of Japan]] coast, and the 10th largest in the country. It was discovered only in 1998, and was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] from 1998 to 2001. No ''[[haniwa]]'' or ''[[fukiishi]]'' were discovered , and the tumulus was found to have been robbed at some time in antiquity. The time of its construction is estimated to date from the end of the 3rd to the late 4th century, and there is no indication of who was buried in this tomb. Keyhole-shaped tombs are commonly attributed to a connection with the Yamato kingdom, whereas "two conjoined rectangle"-type tumuli are found more commonly in western Japan, where there is a connection to the [[Izumo Province|Izumo Kingdom]]. From its size and its location near an ancient port, it has been speculated that this was the tomb of a powerful chief with connections through trade to both polities. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
*Total length: 107.5 meters *Main Portion: 2-stages, 54 meter length x 53 meter width x 10 meter height *Keyway Portion: 2-stages, 53.5 meter length x 49 meter width x 6 meter height
The tumulus was constructed by partly cutting away the ground on a narrow terrace, such that the embankment volume is about 60% of the total volume of the tumulus. It is orientated to the north-northwest. The tumulus was surrounded by an amorphous moat measuring 5 to 18 meters wide and 1.2 to 1.2 meters deep. The tomb appears to be built on the site of a [[Yayoi period]] settlement, as [[Yayoi pottery]] has been found in the area. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
There is a smaller (25 meter diameter) {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} (円墳) surrounded by a five-meter wide and 1.6 meter deep moat is located on the eastern rear side of the tomb. Details of its construction are unknown as it has not been excavated. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The area is now open to the public as part of an [[archaeological park]] with a small museum. It is about 10 minutes by car from [[Himi Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Himi Line]]. <ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun}} | name = Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun | native_name = 柳田布尾山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Nunoo zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Toyama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|49|24|N|136|59|27|E||display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Himi, Toyama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Hokuriku region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th or early 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = 1998-2001 | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park and museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun'''|柳田布尾山古墳|}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]] located in the Yanaida neighborhood of the city of [[Himi, Toyama]] in the [[Hokuriku region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus has been protected as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 2001.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/140287 |title=柳田布尾山古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=ja|access-date=25 December 2017}}</ref>
==Outline== The Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun is located in the northwest part of Toyama prefecture, near the eastern base of [[Noto Peninsula]] and approximately two kilometers from the shore of [[Toyama Bay]], at an elevation of approximately 25 meters above sea level. With a length of 107.5 meters, it is the largest "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}''|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} on the [[Sea of Japan]] coast, and the 10th largest in the country. It was discovered only in 1998, and was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] from 1998 to 2001. No ''[[haniwa]]'' or ''[[fukiishi]]'' were discovered , and the tumulus was found to have been robbed at some time in antiquity. The time of its construction is estimated to date from the end of the 3rd to the late 4th century, and there is no indication of who was buried in this tomb. Keyhole-shaped tombs are commonly attributed to a connection with the Yamato kingdom, whereas "two conjoined rectangle"-type tumuli are found more commonly in western Japan, where there is a connection to the [[Izumo Province|Izumo Kingdom]]. From its size and its location near an ancient port, it has been speculated that this was the tomb of a powerful chief with connections through trade to both polities. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
*Total length: 107.5 meters *Main Portion: 2-stages, 54 meter length x 53 meter width x 10 meter height *Keyway Portion: 2-stages, 53.5 meter length x 49 meter width x 6 meter height
The tumulus was constructed by partly cutting away the ground on a narrow terrace, such that the embankment volume is about 60% of the total volume of the tumulus. It is orientated to the north-northwest. The tumulus was surrounded by an amorphous moat measuring 5 to 18 meters wide and 1.2 to 1.2 meters deep. The tomb appears to be built on the site of a [[Yayoi period]] settlement, as [[Yayoi pottery]] has been found in the area. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
There is a smaller (25 meter diameter) {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=circular tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} (円墳) surrounded by a five-meter wide and 1.6 meter deep moat is located on the eastern rear side of the tomb. Details of its construction are unknown as it has not been excavated. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The area is now open to the public as part of an [[archaeological park]] with a small museum. It is about 10 minutes by car from [[Himi Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Himi Line]]. <ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun}} | name = Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun | native_name = 柳田布尾山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Nunoo zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Toyama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|49|24|N|136|59|27|E||display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Himi, Toyama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Hokuriku region]] | type = [[kofun]] | Group = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th or early 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = 1998-2001 | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park and museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun'''|柳田布尾山古墳|}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]] located in the Yanaida neighborhood of the city of [[Himi, Toyama]] in the [[Hokuriku region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus has been protected as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 2001.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/140287 |title=柳田布尾山古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=ja|access-date=25 December 2017}}</ref>
==Outline== The Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun is located in the northwest part of Toyama prefecture, near the eastern base of [[Noto Peninsula]] and approximately two kilometers from the shore of [[Toyama Bay]], at an elevation of approximately 25 meters above sea level. With a length of 107.5 meters, it is the largest "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}''|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} on the [[Sea of Japan]] coast, and the 10th largest in the country. It was discovered only in 1998, and was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] from 1998 to 2001. No ''[[haniwa]]'' or ''[[fukiishi]]'' were discovered , and the tumulus was found to have been robbed at some time in antiquity. The time of its construction is estimated to date from the end of the 3rd to the late 4th century, and there is no indication of who was buried in this tomb. Keyhole-shaped tombs are commonly attributed to a connection with the Yamato kingdom, whereas "two conjoined rectangle"-type tumuli are found more commonly in western Japan, where there is a connection to the [[Izumo Province|Izumo Kingdom]]. From its size and its location near an ancient port, it has been speculated that this was the tomb of a powerful chief with connections through trade to both polities. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
*Total length: 107.5 meters *Main Portion: 2-stages, 54 meter length x 53 meter width x 10 meter height *Keyway Portion: 2-stages, 53.5 meter length x 49 meter width x 6 meter height
The tumulus was constructed by partly cutting away the ground on a narrow terrace, such that the embankment volume is about 60% of the total volume of the tumulus. It is orientated to the north-northwest. The tumulus was surrounded by an amorphous moat measuring 5 to 18 meters wide and 1.2 to 1.2 meters deep. The tomb appears to be built on the site of a [[Yayoi period]] settlement, as [[Yayoi pottery]] has been found in the area. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
There is a smaller (25 meter diameter) {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=circular tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} (円墳) surrounded by a five-meter wide and 1.6 meter deep moat is located on the eastern rear side of the tomb. Details of its construction are unknown as it has not been excavated. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The area is now open to the public as part of an [[archaeological park]] with a small museum. It is about 10 minutes by car from [[Himi Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Himi Line]]. <ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun}} | name = Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun | native_name = 柳田布尾山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Nunoo zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Toyama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|49|24|N|136|59|27|E||display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Himi, Toyama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Hokuriku region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th or early 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = 1998-2001 | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park and museum) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun'''|柳田布尾山古墳|}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]] located in the Yanaida neighborhood of the city of [[Himi, Toyama]] in the [[Hokuriku region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus has been protected as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 2001.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/140287 |title=柳田布尾山古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=ja|access-date=25 December 2017}}</ref>
==Outline== The Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun is located in the northwest part of Toyama prefecture, near the eastern base of [[Noto Peninsula]] and approximately two kilometers from the shore of [[Toyama Bay]], at an elevation of approximately 25 meters above sea level. With a length of 107.5 meters, it is the largest "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}''|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} on the [[Sea of Japan]] coast, and the 10th largest in the country. It was discovered only in 1998, and was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] from 1998 to 2001. No ''[[haniwa]]'' or ''[[fukiishi]]'' were discovered , and the tumulus was found to have been robbed at some time in antiquity. The time of its construction is estimated to date from the end of the 3rd to the late 4th century, and there is no indication of who was buried in this tomb. Keyhole-shaped tombs are commonly attributed to a connection with the Yamato kingdom, whereas "two conjoined rectangle"-type tumuli are found more commonly in western Japan, where there is a connection to the [[Izumo Province|Izumo Kingdom]]. From its size and its location near an ancient port, it has been speculated that this was the tomb of a powerful chief with connections through trade to both polities. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
*Total length: 107.5 meters *Main Portion: 2-stages, 54 meter length x 53 meter width x 10 meter height *Keyway Portion: 2-stages, 53.5 meter length x 49 meter width x 6 meter height
The tumulus was constructed by partly cutting away the ground on a narrow terrace, such that the embankment volume is about 60% of the total volume of the tumulus. It is orientated to the north-northwest. The tumulus was surrounded by an amorphous moat measuring 5 to 18 meters wide and 1.2 to 1.2 meters deep. The tomb appears to be built on the site of a [[Yayoi period]] settlement, as [[Yayoi pottery]] has been found in the area. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
There is a smaller (25 meter diameter) {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=circular tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} (円墳) surrounded by a five-meter wide and 1.6 meter deep moat is located on the eastern rear side of the tomb. Details of its construction are unknown as it has not been excavated. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The area is now open to the public as part of an [[archaeological park]] with a small museum. It is about 10 minutes by car from [[Himi Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Himi Line]]. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun, kouhoubu.jpg|From front looking toward rear File:Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|From rear looking towards front File:Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun, rikkyo.jpg|Bridge across moat on east side File:Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun-2, zenkei.jpg|No.2 tumulus </gallery>

{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=draft}} | name = Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun | native_name = 柳田布尾山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Nunoo zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Toyama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|49|24|N|136|59|27|E||display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Himi, Toyama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Hokuriku region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th or early 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = 1998-2001 | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park and museum) | Historic Site = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun'''|柳田布尾山古墳|}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]] located in the Yanaida neighborhood of the city of [[Himi, Toyama]] in the [[Hokuriku region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus has been protected as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 2001.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/140287 |title=柳田布尾山古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=ja|access-date=25 December 2017}}</ref>
==Outline== The Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun is located in the northwest part of Toyama prefecture, near the eastern base of [[Noto Peninsula]] and approximately two kilometers from the shore of [[Toyama Bay]], at an elevation of approximately 25 meters above sea level. With a length of 107.5 meters, it is the largest "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=draft}}''|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} on the [[Sea of Japan]] coast, and the 10th largest in the country. It was discovered only in 1998, and was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] from 1998 to 2001. No ''[[haniwa]]'' or ''[[fukiishi]]'' were discovered , and the tumulus was found to have been robbed at some time in antiquity. The time of its construction is estimated to date from the end of the 3rd to the late 4th century, and there is no indication of who was buried in this tomb. Keyhole-shaped tombs are commonly attributed to a connection with the Yamato kingdom, whereas "two conjoined rectangle"-type tumuli are found more commonly in western Japan, where there is a connection to the [[Izumo Province|Izumo Kingdom]]. From its size and its location near an ancient port, it has been speculated that this was the tomb of a powerful chief with connections through trade to both polities. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
*Total length: 107.5 meters *Main Portion: 2-stages, 54 meter length x 53 meter width x 10 meter height *Keyway Portion: 2-stages, 53.5 meter length x 49 meter width x 6 meter height
The tumulus was constructed by partly cutting away the ground on a narrow terrace, such that the embankment volume is about 60% of the total volume of the tumulus. It is orientated to the north-northwest. The tumulus was surrounded by an amorphous moat measuring 5 to 18 meters wide and 1.2 to 1.2 meters deep. The tomb appears to be built on the site of a [[Yayoi period]] settlement, as [[Yayoi pottery]] has been found in the area. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
There is a smaller (25 meter diameter) {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=circular tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} (円墳) surrounded by a five-meter wide and 1.6 meter deep moat is located on the eastern rear side of the tomb. Details of its construction are unknown as it has not been excavated. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The area is now open to the public as part of an [[archaeological park]] with a small museum. It is about 10 minutes by car from [[Himi Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Himi Line]]. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun, kouhoubu.jpg|From front looking toward rear File:Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|From rear looking towards front File:Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun, rikkyo.jpg|Bridge across moat on east side File:Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun-2, zenkei.jpg|No.2 tumulus </gallery>
==See also== {{Portal|History|Japan}} *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Toyama)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category|Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun}} * [https://www.info-toyama.com/spot/21018/ Toyama Tourist Information] {{in lang|ja}} * [http://www.city.himi.toyama.jp/shimin/node_26855/node_1342/node_1602/node_27824 Himi City official site] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://toyama-bunkaisan.jp/search/1651/ Cultural Heritage of Toyama] {{in lang|ja}}

{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=draft}} | name = Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun | native_name = 柳田布尾山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Nunoo zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Toyama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|49|24|N|136|59|27|E||display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Himi, Toyama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Hokuriku region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th or early 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = 1998-2001 | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park and museum) | Historic Site = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun'''|柳田布尾山古墳|}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]] located in the Yanaida neighborhood of the city of [[Himi, Toyama]] in the [[Hokuriku region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus has been protected as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 2001.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/140287 |title=柳田布尾山古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=ja|access-date=25 December 2017}}</ref>
==Outline== The Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun is located in the northwest part of Toyama prefecture, near the eastern base of [[Noto Peninsula]] and approximately two kilometers from the shore of [[Toyama Bay]], at an elevation of approximately 25 meters above sea level. With a length of 107.5 meters, it is the largest "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=draft}}''|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} on the [[Sea of Japan]] coast, and the 10th largest in the country. It was discovered only in 1998, and was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] from 1998 to 2001. No ''[[haniwa]]'' or ''[[fukiishi]]'' were discovered , and the tumulus was found to have been robbed at some time in antiquity. The time of its construction is estimated to date from the end of the 3rd to the late 4th century, and there is no indication of who was buried in this tomb. Keyhole-shaped tombs are commonly attributed to a connection with the Yamato kingdom, whereas "two conjoined rectangle"-type tumuli are found more commonly in western Japan, where there is a connection to the [[Izumo Province|Izumo Kingdom]]. From its size and its location near an ancient port, it has been speculated that this was the tomb of a powerful chief with connections through trade to both polities. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
*Total length: 107.5 meters *Main Portion: 2-stages, 54 meter length x 53 meter width x 10 meter height *Keyway Portion: 2-stages, 53.5 meter length x 49 meter width x 6 meter height
The tumulus was constructed by partly cutting away the ground on a narrow terrace, such that the embankment volume is about 60% of the total volume of the tumulus. It is orientated to the north-northwest. The tumulus was surrounded by an amorphous moat measuring 5 to 18 meters wide and 1.2 to 1.2 meters deep. The tomb appears to be built on the site of a [[Yayoi period]] settlement, as [[Yayoi pottery]] has been found in the area. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
There is a smaller (25 meter diameter) {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=circular tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} (円墳) surrounded by a five-meter wide and 1.6 meter deep moat is located on the eastern rear side of the tomb. Details of its construction are unknown as it has not been excavated. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The area is now open to the public as part of an [[archaeological park]] with a small museum. It is about 10 minutes by car from [[Himi Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Himi Line]]. <ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | name = Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun | native_name = 柳田布尾山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Nunoo zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Toyama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|49|24|N|136|59|27|E||display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Himi, Toyama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Hokuriku region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th or early 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = 1998-2001 | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park and museum) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun'''|柳田布尾山古墳|}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]] located in the Yanaida neighborhood of the city of [[Himi, Toyama]] in the [[Hokuriku region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus has been protected as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 2001.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/140287 |title=柳田布尾山古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=ja|access-date=25 December 2017}}</ref>
==Outline== The Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun is located in the northwest part of Toyama prefecture, near the eastern base of [[Noto Peninsula]] and approximately two kilometers from the shore of [[Toyama Bay]], at an elevation of approximately 25 meters above sea level. With a length of 107.5 meters, it is the largest "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} on the [[Sea of Japan]] coast, and the 10th largest in the country. It was discovered only in 1998, and was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] from 1998 to 2001. No ''[[haniwa]]'' or ''[[fukiishi]]'' were discovered , and the tumulus was found to have been robbed at some time in antiquity. The time of its construction is estimated to date from the end of the 3rd to the late 4th century, and there is no indication of who was buried in this tomb. Keyhole-shaped tombs are commonly attributed to a connection with the Yamato kingdom, whereas "two conjoined rectangle"-type tumuli are found more commonly in western Japan, where there is a connection to the [[Izumo Province|Izumo Kingdom]]. From its size and its location near an ancient port, it has been speculated that this was the tomb of a powerful chief with connections through trade to both polities. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
*Total length: 107.5 meters *Main Portion: 2-stages, 54 meter length x 53 meter width x 10 meter height *Keyway Portion: 2-stages, 53.5 meter length x 49 meter width x 6 meter height
The tumulus was constructed by partly cutting away the ground on a narrow terrace, such that the embankment volume is about 60% of the total volume of the tumulus. It is orientated to the north-northwest. The tumulus was surrounded by an amorphous moat measuring 5 to 18 meters wide and 1.2 to 1.2 meters deep. The tomb appears to be built on the site of a [[Yayoi period]] settlement, as [[Yayoi pottery]] has been found in the area. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
There is a smaller (25 meter diameter) {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} (円墳) surrounded by a five-meter wide and 1.6 meter deep moat is located on the eastern rear side of the tomb. Details of its construction are unknown as it has not been excavated. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The area is now open to the public as part of an [[archaeological park]] with a small museum. It is about 10 minutes by car from [[Himi Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Himi Line]]. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun, kouhoubu.jpg|From front looking toward rear File:Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|From rear looking towards front File:Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun, rikkyo.jpg|Bridge across moat on east side File:Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun-2, zenkei.jpg|No.2 tumulus </gallery>
==See also== {{Portal|History|Japan}} *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Toyama)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category|Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun}} * [https://www.info-toyama.com/spot/21018/ Toyama Tourist Information] {{in lang|ja}} * [http://www.city.himi.toyama.jp/shimin/node_26855/node_1342/node_1602/node_27824 Himi City official site] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://toyama-bunkaisan.jp/search/1651/ Cultural Heritage of Toyama] {{in lang|ja}}

With a length of 107.5 meters, it is the largest "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus {{nihongo|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=}}''|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} on the [[Sea of Japan]] coast, and the 10th largest in the country. It was discovered only in 1998, and was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] from 1998 to 2001. No ''[[haniwa]]'' or ''[[fukiishi]]'' were discovered , and the tumulus was found to have been robbed at some time in antiquity. The time of its construction is estimated to date from the end of the 3rd to the late 4th century, and there is no indication of who was buried in this tomb. Keyhole-shaped tombs are commonly attributed to a connection with the Yamato kingdom, whereas "two conjoined rectangle"-type tumuli are found more commonly in western Japan, where there is a connection to the [[Izumo Province|Izumo Kingdom]]. From its size and its location near an ancient port, it has been speculated that this was the tomb of a powerful chief with connections through trade to both polities. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
*Total length: 107.5 meters *Main Portion: 2-stages, 54 meter length x 53 meter width x 10 meter height *Keyway Portion: 2-stages, 53.5 meter length x 49 meter width x 6 meter height
The tumulus was constructed by partly cutting away the ground on a narrow terrace, such that the embankment volume is about 60% of the total volume of the tumulus. It is orientated to the north-northwest. The tumulus was surrounded by an amorphous moat measuring 5 to 18 meters wide and 1.2 to 1.2 meters deep. The tomb appears to be built on the site of a [[Yayoi period]] settlement, as [[Yayoi pottery]] has been found in the area. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
There is a smaller (25 meter diameter) {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|qq=}} (円墳) surrounded by a five-meter wide and 1.6 meter deep moat is located on the eastern rear side of the tomb. Details of its construction are unknown as it has not been excavated. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The area is now open to the public as part of an [[archaeological park]] with a small museum. It is about 10 minutes by car from [[Himi Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Himi Line]]. <ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=}} | name = Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun | native_name = 柳田布尾山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Nunoo zenkei.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Toyama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|49|24|N|136|59|27|E||display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Himi, Toyama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Hokuriku region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th or early 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = 1998-2001 | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park and museum) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun'''|柳田布尾山古墳|}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]] located in the Yanaida neighborhood of the city of [[Himi, Toyama]] in the [[Hokuriku region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus has been protected as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] since 2001.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/140287 |title=柳田布尾山古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=ja|access-date=25 December 2017}}</ref>
==Outline== The Yanaida Nunōyama Kofun is located in the northwest part of Toyama prefecture, near the eastern base of [[Noto Peninsula]] and approximately two kilometers from the shore of [[Toyama Bay]], at an elevation of approximately 25 meters above sea level. With a length of 107.5 meters, it is the largest "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus {{ill|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because of the mass replacement error that gets rid of the links like this otherwise|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=}}''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} on the [[Sea of Japan]] coast, and the 10th largest in the country. It was discovered only in 1998, and was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] from 1998 to 2001. No ''[[haniwa]]'' or ''[[fukiishi]]'' were discovered , and the tumulus was found to have been robbed at some time in antiquity. The time of its construction is estimated to date from the end of the 3rd to the late 4th century, and there is no indication of who was buried in this tomb. Keyhole-shaped tombs are commonly attributed to a connection with the Yamato kingdom, whereas "two conjoined rectangle"-type tumuli are found more commonly in western Japan, where there is a connection to the [[Izumo Province|Izumo Kingdom]]. From its size and its location near an ancient port, it has been speculated that this was the tomb of a powerful chief with connections through trade to both polities. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
*Total length: 107.5 meters *Main Portion: 2-stages, 54 meter length x 53 meter width x 10 meter height *Keyway Portion: 2-stages, 53.5 meter length x 49 meter width x 6 meter height
The tumulus was constructed by partly cutting away the ground on a narrow terrace, such that the embankment volume is about 60% of the total volume of the tumulus. It is orientated to the north-northwest. The tumulus was surrounded by an amorphous moat measuring 5 to 18 meters wide and 1.2 to 1.2 meters deep. The tomb appears to be built on the site of a [[Yayoi period]] settlement, as [[Yayoi pottery]] has been found in the area. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
There is a smaller (25 meter diameter) {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|qq=}} (円墳) surrounded by a five-meter wide and 1.6 meter deep moat is located on the eastern rear side of the tomb. Details of its construction are unknown as it has not been excavated. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The area is now open to the public as part of an [[archaeological park]] with a small museum. It is about 10 minutes by car from [[Himi Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Himi Line]]. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun, kouhoubu.jpg|From front looking toward rear File:Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|From rear looking towards front File:Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun, rikkyo.jpg|Bridge across moat on east side File:Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun-2, zenkei.jpg|No.2 tumulus </gallery>
==See also== {{Portal|History|Japan}} *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Toyama)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category|Yanaida Nunooyama Kofun}} * [https://www.info-toyama.com/spot/21018/ Toyama Tourist Information] {{in lang|ja}} * [http://www.city.himi.toyama.jp/shimin/node_26855/node_1342/node_1602/node_27824 Himi City official site] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://toyama-bunkaisan.jp/search/1651/ Cultural Heritage of Toyama] {{in lang|ja}}

*Total length: 107.5 meters *Main Portion: 2-stages, 54 meter length x 53 meter width x 10 meter height *Keyway Portion: 2-stages, 53.5 meter length x 49 meter width x 6 meter height
The tumulus was constructed by partly cutting away the ground on a narrow terrace, such that the embankment volume is about 60% of the total volume of the tumulus. It is orientated to the north-northwest. The tumulus was surrounded by an amorphous moat measuring 5 to 18 meters wide and 1.2 to 1.2 meters deep. The tomb appears to be built on the site of a [[Yayoi period]] settlement, as [[Yayoi pottery]] has been found in the area. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
There is a smaller (25 meter diameter) {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (円墳) surrounded by a five-meter wide and 1.6 meter deep moat is located on the eastern rear side of the tomb. Details of its construction are unknown as it has not been excavated. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
The area is now open to the public as part of an [[archaeological park]] with a small museum. It is about 10 minutes by car from [[Himi Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Himi Line]]. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
